svg.text: so it whirls — some young neutron stars rotating hundreds of times every second.
The collapse concentrates the star's magnetic field as fiercely as its spin,
amplifying it to trillions of times the strength of any magnet built by human
hands. A spinning magnet radiates, and a neutron star beams energy from its mag-
netic poles in two narrow cones. If one of those beams happens to sweep across
the Earth, we see a pulse with every rotation — as regular as a ticking clock.
Little Green Men, 1967
When Jocelyn Bell Burnell first recorded a string of impossibly regular pulses
on her radio telescope, the signal was so precise that the source was
half-jokingly labelled ‘LGM-1’, for Little Green Men. The pulses turned out to be
natural: the lighthouse beam of a rotating neutron star, the first pulsar ever
found.
These beaming neutron stars, or pulsars, come in a remarkable variety:
• Radio pulsars sweep lighthouse beams of radio waves across space with
metronomic regularity, and serve as some of the most precise clocks in
the universe.
• Millisecond pulsars have been ‘recycled’ — spun up to hundreds of
rotations per second by matter stripped from a companion star.
• Magnetars carry the most intense magnetic fields known, occasionally
convulsing in titanic bursts of X-rays and gamma rays.
• X-ray binaries blaze in X-rays as gas torn from an orbiting star
spirals down and crashes onto the neutron star's surface.
Laboratories at the Edge of
Physics
Precisely because their interiors lie so far beyond our reach, neutron stars
have become indispensable instruments for testing the laws of nature. Their
fierce gravity bends light and slows time in ways that confirm Einstein's gen-
eral relativity to extraordinary precision, and the steady drumbeat of pulsars
lets astronomers weigh distant stars and hunt for ripples in spacetime itself.
In 2017 the field reached a milestone when two neutron stars, locked in a
death spiral, were observed merging — first as a chirp of gravitational waves
and moments later as a blaze of light. The collision flung out a cloud of de-
bris glowing as a kilonova, and within it astronomers watched heavy elements
being forged in real time.
Where Your Gold Came From
The merger of 2017 helped settle a long-standing mystery: where the universe's
